content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 ,J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
John A. Nagel 
City Attorney 
456 West Olive Avenue 
Sunnyvale, CA 94088 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-101 
Dear Mr. Nagel: 
June 28, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Sunnyvale City Councilmembers 
and Successor Agency to the Sunnyvale Redevelopment Agency ("Successor Agency") Board 
Members Gustav Larsson and Jim Davis regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common 
law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Under the Act, may Sunnyvale City Councilmembers and Successor Agency Board 
Members Larsson and Davis (hereafter "Councilmember Larsson" and "Councilmember Davis,'' 
respectively, the "Councilmembers," collectively) take part in governmental decisions regarding 
proposed amendments to existing contractual agreements and land use entitlements related to the 
Town Center Project, a mixed use development located within Block 18 of the City's Downtown 
Sunnyvale Specific Plan ("DSP")? 
CONCLUSION 
The Councilmembers may take part in the following potential City Council and Successor 
Agency decisions: ( 1) whether to modify the Master Special Development Permit ("SOP") to reflect 
changes in the Building Code, (2) whether to modify the SOP to reflect the currents status of the 
Project, (3) whether to modify the liquidated damages provisions of the "2010 Amended 
Disposition and Development and Owner Participation Agreement" ("ADDOPA"), and (4) whether 
to delete the developer's entitlement to annual Tax Increment Financing payments under the 
ADDOPA, so long as those decisions do not reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the previous 
decisions approving the Project, the DSP, the development plan for Block 18. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-101 
Page No. 2 
However, the Councilmembers may not take part in any major policy decisions regarding 
proposed amendments to existing contractual agreements and land use entitlements related to the 
Project because they are "inextricably interrelated" to the original approvals of the Project, the DSP, 
or the development plan for Block 18. Therefore, those decisions may not be segmented, and the 
Act prohibits the Councilmembers from taking part in them. 
FACTS 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Sunnyvale, and the authorized representative of 
Councilmembers Larsson and Davis. Councilmember Larsson owns his residence approximately 
863 feet southeast, and Councilmember Davis owns his residence approximately 1,052 feet due 
east, from the southeast comer of the Town Center Project and Block 18 of the DSP. 
The City originally adopted the DSP in 1993, and substantially revised it in 2003. The DSP 
comprises roughly 125 acres, which are subdivided into blocks. Each block has its own 
development standards and regulations. Block 18 is located on the site of the former Town Center 
Mall, and was a redevelopment plan area of the former Sunnyvale Redevelopment Agency 
("SRA"). Many of the other blocks of the DSP have since been developed. 
In 2007 and 2008, the City and the SRA approved a plan, the Town Center Project, to 
demolish the mall and construct a mixed use project in its place. The Project would include 292 for­
sale residential units, 315,000 square feet of office space, 200 hotel rooms, and 634,000 square feet 
of retail space, including a multiplex cinema, restaurants, a recreation facility, and outdoor uses. 
Construction started in 2008, and was halted midway when the developer's financing failed. The 
Project was eventually taken over by its lender, Wachovia Bank (later acquired by Wells Fargo 
Bank), via foreclosure. Completion of the Project was further delayed by several years of litigation 
between the former developer, Wells Fargo Bank, and other parties. The litigation was finally 
resolved in 2015. 
Wells Fargo Bank recently informed the City that it intends to sell the Project to a new 
developer. The Successor Agency approved the qualifications of the prospective development team 
in December 2015, and City staff are currently in negotiations with the new developers over certain 
updates and modifications to the various approvals and agreements governing the Project. 
The City granted the Master Special Development Permit ("SDP") for the Project in 2007, 
and it remains in effect. The developers have proposed the following amendments to the SDP: { l )  
modifying the SDP to reflect changes in the City's Below Market Rate Housing obligations, 
(2) updating the SDP to allow for rental housing units rather than for-sale housing units, 
(3) updating the SDP to bring it into compliance with the current Building Code, and (4) updating 
the SDP to reflect the current status of the Project. 
In 20 I 0, the Successor Agency and the developer entered into the "20 l O Amended 
Disposition and Development and Owner Participation Agreement" ("ADDOPA")2 that provides 
for the completion and construction of the Project, describes the required scope and mix of land 
uses, and specifies the respective duties and obligations of the developer and the Successor Agency. 
2 An owner panicipation agreement is a legal mechanism to regulate development in a redevelopment area. 


File No. A-16-101 
Page No. 3 
Among other things, the ADDOPA sets forth the developer's liability for liquidated 
damages for failing to complete the Project, the developer's obligations under the City's Below 
Market Rate Housing requirements, the types of housing units to be included in the Project, and the 
developer's entitlement to Tax Increment Financing3 ("TIF"). The City and the new developers for 
the Project are in the process of considering amendments to these provisions of the ADDO PA. 
Currently, Block 18 includes two completed office buildings (fully leased to Nokia and 
Apple) and Target and Macy's stores with associated parking structures and surface parking. There 
are also several partially constructed buildings, a non-operational parking structure, and a surface 
parking area where a hotel, additional retail space, and a parking structure are planned. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Under Section 87103(b ), an official has a financial interest in a decision within 
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. . 
Councilmember Larsson 
Your office originally sought advice regarding whether the Act prohibited Councilmember 
Larsson from taking part in decisions related to the approval of a new developer for the Project. We 
concluded in our July 28, 2015 advice that due to the "scope, intensity, and proximity" of the 
Project to Councilmember Larsson's home, he has a "conflict in decisions related to the DSP and 
redevelopment of Block 18, and may not make, participate in making, or use his official position to 
influence these decisions." (Borger Advice Letter, No. 1-15-110.) 
Thereafter, your office sought further advice regarding whether the Act prohibited 
Councilmember Larsson from taking part in decisions related to timing and phasing of construction 
financing, extension of time frames, and ownership structure of the Project's housing units. In our 
December 2, 2015 advice, we affirmed our prior conclusion that Section 87100 prohibits 
Councilmember Larsson from taking part in decisions related to the Project, the DSP, or the 
development plan for Block 18. (See Borger Advice Letter, No. A-15-226.) 
We again affirm our conclusion that Section 87100 prohibits Councilmember Larsson from 
taking part in decisions related to the Project, the DSP, or the development plan for Block 18. 
l Tax Increment Financing is a way for developers to share in the increased property tax revenues generated by 
a redevelopment proJect. TIF ended in 2011 when the State dissolved redevelopment agencies. The ADDOP A provides 
that the developer will receive TIF payments upon completion of a minimum phase of the Project. Because the former 
developer failed to complete the Project within the required time frames, it is unclear whether a new developer could 
establish a right to TIF. Any payment ofTIF money would depend on, among other things, whether the ADDOPA 
creates an enforceable obligation to pay TIF under the Redevelopment Agency Dissolution law (Health and Safety 
Code, Division 24, Parts l.8 and 1.85 (commencing with Section 34161)). 


File No. A-16-101 
Page No. 4 
Because the decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
Councilmember Larsson 's real property interest in his residence, located approximately 863 feet 
from the southeast comer of the Project, Section 87100 prohibits Councilmember Larsson from 
taking part in those decisions. 
Councilmember Davis 
Councilmember Davis owns his residence approximately 1,052 feet due east from the 
southeast corner of the Project. Although Councilmember Davis's residence is an additional 189 
feet away from the Project than Councilmember Larsson's residence, you have not provided any 
evidence indicating that the additional distance away from the Project would effectively mitigate 
the impacts on Councilmember Davis's residence resultant from the scope, intensity, and proximity 
of the Project. Therefore, the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on Councilmember Davis's real property interest in his residence. 
Thus, the Section 87100 prohibits both Councilmembers Larsson and Davis from taking part 
in decisions relating related to the Project, the DSP, or the development plan for Block 18. 
Segmentation 
_ Regulation 18706 identifies circumstances in which an ,agency may segment governmental 
decisions. In some cases, "some large, complex decisions may be segmented into separate decisions 
so that even if an official has a disqualifying interest in one component of the series of decisions, he 
may still participate in other components in which he has no financial interest." 
Regulation 18706 requires that all of the following must apply: 
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down into 
separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in which the official 
has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is se1:,JJ11ented from the other 
decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first and a final 
decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's participation in any 
way; and 
"( 4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, the 
disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or otherwise 
financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified." 
Where a decision in which an official has a conflict of interest is "inextricably interrelated" 
to another decision, the official will be required to disqualify from both decisions. Regulation 
18706(b) provides that "decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision will 
effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 


Previous Advice 
File No. A-16-101 
Page No. 5 
Applying the Act's segmentation rules in our previous advice on these decisions, we 
detennined that Councilmember Larsson may take part in certain decisions "so long as they do not 
reopen, detennine, affinn, nullify, or alter the previous decisions approving the DSP, the 
development plan for Block 18, or any other decision from which he may be disqualified." (Borger 
Advice Letter, No. A-15-226; B_orger Advice Letter, No. 1-15-110.) 
Specifically, we concluded that Councilmember Larsson may participate in the decision on 
whether to approve a new developer for the Project (Borger Advice Letter, No. 1-15-110), and 
decisions regarding timing and phasing of construction of the Project (Borger Advice Letter, No. A-
15-226), subject to the above described condition. 
However, we also concluded that decisions regarding the construction financing, extension 
ohime frames, and the ownership structure of housing units are "inextricably interrelated" to the 
original approvals of the DSP or the development plan for Block 18. (Id. at p. 8.) We expressly 
noted that "this conclusion applies to all major policy decisions" that could reopen, detennine, 
affinn, nullify, or alter the previous approvals of the Project, the DSP, or the development plan for 
Block 18 (id. at p. 9.) Therefore, the Act prohibited Councilmember Larsson from taking part in 
those decisions. 
We affinn our previous advice regarding whether these decisions may be segmented. All 
major policy decisions that could reopen, detennine, affinn, nullify, or alter the previous approvals 
of the Project, the DSP, or the development plan for Block 18 may not be segmented. 
You now seek additional advice about whether certain decisions regarding proposed 
amendments to existing contractual agreements and land use entitlements governing the Project 
may be segmented. We consider these decisions below. 
Potential City Council Decisions 
The Planning Commission will soon consider whether to approve the following amendments 
to the SDP: (1) the modification to reflect changes in the City's Below Market Rate Housing 
obligations; (2) the modification to allow for rental housing units rather than for-sale housing units; 
(3) amendments to reflect changes in the Building Code; and (4) amendments to update the current 
status of the Project to reflect that some required public improvements having already been 
completed. These Planning Commission decisions would only come before the City Council if they 
are appealed. 
The amendments to reflect changes in the Building Code and to update the current status of 
the Project are ministerial changes that are not "inextricably interrelated" to the original approvals. 
Therefore, these decisions may be segmented, and the Councilmember may take part in them. 
However, the modifications to reflect changes in the City's Below Market Rate Housing 
obligations and to allow the change from for-sale housing units to rental housing units are changes 
that could reopen, detennine, affinn, nullify, or alter the previous approvals of the Project or the 
development plan for Block 18. The manner in which the Project would meet its Below Market 


File No. A-16-101 
Page No. 6 
Rate Housing obligations and the types of housing units to be included in the Project were material 
provisions of the original approvals, heavily negotiated in light of the requirements under then­
existing law. Therefore, these decisions may not be segmented, and the Councilmembers may not 
take part in them. 
Successor Agency Decisions 
The Successor Agency will soon consider whether to approve the following amendments to 
the ADDO PA: (I) the modification of the liquidated damages provisions; (2) the modification to 
allow the developer to meet the City's Below Market Rate Housing obligations by reserving a 
portion of the rental units within the project for low-income households; (3) the modification to 
allow the Project's residential units to be rental housing units rather than for-sale housing units; and 
(4) the deletion of the developer's entitlement to annual TIF payments. 
The modification of the liquidated damages provisions and the deletion of the developer's 
entitlement to annual TIF payments arc not the type of decisions that could reopen, determine, 
affirm, nullify, or alter the original approvals of the Project or the development plan for Block 18. 
The original approvals have already been altered with respect to these decisions due to the 
dissolution of redevelopment agencies, the failure of the Project's original financing, and the 
passage of time. Therefore, these decisions would merely redress the original approvals to account 
for changes in the Project's circumstanc¥s, they are not "inextricably interrelated" to the original 
approvals, and they may be segmented. 
However, the modifications to allow the developer to meet the City's Below Market Rate 
Housing obligations and to allow Project's residential units to be rental housing units are major 
policy decisions that could reopen, determine, affinn, nullify, or alter the original approvals of the 
Project or the development plan for Block 18. As noted above, the manner in which the Project 
would meet its Below Market Rate Housing obligations and the types of housing units to be 
included in the Project were material provisions of the original approvals, heavily negotiated in 
light of the requirements under then-existing law. Therefore, these decisions may not be se!:,rmented, 
and the Councilmembers may not take part in them. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
- 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

Sunnyvale Specific Plan ("DSP")?
sections.conclusion:
The Councilmembers may take part in the following potential City Council and Successor 
Agency decisions: ( 1) whether to modify the Master Special Development Permit ("SOP") to reflect

decisions approving the Project, the DSP, the development plan for Block 18.However, the Councilmembers may not take part in any major policy decisions regarding 
proposed amendments to existing contractual agreements and land use entitlements related to the 
Project because they are "inextricably interrelated" to the original approvals of the Project, the DSP, 
or the development plan for Block 18. Therefore, those decisions may not be segmented, and the 
Act prohibits the Councilmembers from taking part in them.

Section 87100

Members Larsson and Davis (hereafter "Councilmember Larsson" and "Councilmember Davis,''

Sunnyvale Specific Plan ("DSP")?

Agency decisions: ( 1) whether to modify the Master Special Development Permit ("SOP") to reflect 
changes in the Building Code, (2) whether to modify the SOP to reflect the currents status of the 
Project, (3) whether to modify the liquidated damages provisions of the "2010 Amended 
Disposition and Development and Owner Participation Agreement" ("ADDOPA"), and (4) whether 
to delete the developer's entitlement to annual Tax Increment Financing payments under the 
ADDOPA, so long as those decisions do not reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the previous 
decisions approving the Project, the DSP, the development plan for Block 18.However, the Councilmembers may not take part in any major policy decisions regarding 
proposed amendments to existing contractual agreements and land use entitlements related to the 
Project because they are "inextricably interrelated" to the original approvals of the Project, the DSP, 
or the development plan for Block 18. Therefore, those decisions may not be segmented, and the 
Act prohibits the Councilmembers from taking part in them.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 428 ,J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 John A. Nagel City Attorney 456 West Olive Avenue Sunnyvale, CA 94088 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-101 Dear Mr. Nagel: June 28, 2016 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Sunnyvale City Councilmembers and Successor Agency to the Sunnyvale Redevelopment Agency ("Successor Agency") Board Members Gustav Larsson and Jim Davis regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. QUESTION Under the Act, may Sunnyvale City Councilmembers and Successor Agency Board Members Larsson and Davis (hereafter "Councilmember Larsson" and "Councilmember Davis,'' respectively, the "Councilmembers," collectively) take part in governmental decisions regarding proposed amendments to existing contractual agreements and land use entitlements related to the Town Center Project, a mixed use development located within Block 18 of the City's Downtown Sunnyvale Specific Plan ("DSP")? CONCLUSION The Councilmembers may take part in the following potential City Council and Successor Agency decisions: ( 1) whether to modify the Master Special Development Permit ("SOP") to reflect changes in the Building Code, (2) whether to modify the SOP to reflect the currents status of the Project, (3) whether to modify the liquidated damages provisions of the "2010 Amended Disposition and Development and Owner Participation Agreement" ("ADDOPA"), and (4) whether to delete the developer's entitlement to annual Tax Increment Financing payments under the ADDOPA, so long as those decisions do not reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the previous decisions approving the Project, the DSP, the development plan for Block 18. 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-101 Page No. 2 However, the Councilmembers may not take part in any major policy decisions regarding proposed amendments to existing contractual agreements and land use entitlements related to the Project because they are "inextricably interrelated" to the original approvals of the Project, the DSP, or the development plan for Block 18. Therefore, those decisions may not be segmented, and the Act prohibits the Councilmembers from taking part in them. FACTS You are the City Attorney for the City of Sunnyvale, and the authorized representative of Councilmembers Larsson and Davis. Councilmember Larsson owns his residence approximately 863 feet southeast, and Councilmember Davis owns his residence approximately 1,052 feet due east, from the southeast comer of the Town Center Project and Block 18 of the
